column2: Jung, Jo; See, Harrison W.; Green, Lelia
column43: ‘Experience’ is at the core of how we interact with our digital and material worlds. In this issue, the notion of ‘experience’ is examined through engagement with digital media and culture. As contemporary lifestyles continue to blur the distinction between digital and material spaces, hybrid experiences are becoming ubiquitous as access to real-world events and services often requires an accompanying app or online profile. Human experience is now firmly fashioned by our use of digital platforms, distorting connections between what we know, what we feel and what we experience. Digital engagement affords opportunities and risks, yet, as the papers in this issue of MC Journal show, balancing disadvantages is becoming increasingly challenging. Indeed, generative AI is complicating the ‘appearance’ of experience, while also reshaping the aesthetic of our everyday culture.Convincing text, image, and now video can be generated by amateur practitioners with little more than an Internet connection, creating faux experiences that can, nonetheless, have visceral effects on audiences. This is happening even as human labour, creativity, and knowledge can be devalued by prioritising efficiency in the workplace. A fear of negative online experiences had led to the imminent exclusion of Australians under 16 from social media accounts. Yet the increasing accessibility and sophistication of AI chatbots make them more rewarding for some adults than human interaction, and this attracts little attention. As AI itself becomes yet more pervasive and opaque, it’s only appropriate to speculate as to whether, or when, it may itself become able to experience…The feature article by Jo Jung examines visual rhetoric in AI design, linking this to users’ experiences with everyday technologies such as lane departure warnings in contemporary cars and fitness apps that track personal biometrics and prompt healthy choices. Jung argues that the overarching message of many of these encounters with complex computation, sensors, and trackers is the reassuring mundanity of ‘I belong here: I will not intrude’. The user experience—the UX—of such services is routinely positioned as a helpful part of daily life, anticipating and reacting on our behalf: keeping us safe. Such perceptions are an outcome of corporate strategy and conscious design choices that obscure the complexity, and the human and planetary costs, of embedding AI within more and more of the daily services we take for granted.In our second article, Chris Kueh examines an emerging, experiential approach to service design (SD) that goes beyond understanding users’ lived experiences through pragmatic touchpoints or journey maps. Kueh poses a more embodied and emotional SD framework that takes a philosophical approach, integrating theories of phenomenology, existentialism, and aesthetics to better support users’ sense of agency and identity. Indeed, Kueh’s proposition is that these theories can be applied in practical ways to real-world design contexts. Arguing for the value of empathetic and grounded design practices, Kueh offers a nuanced perspective on how SD can emphasise transformative and human-centred user experiences, thereby providing a response to a Redditor’s lament: “the internet used to be an escape from the real world. Now the real world is an escape from the internet.”Keeping within the philosophical trajectory, Alessandro D'Aloia uses our third article to ask whether AI can ‘experience’ its engagement with media and culture. He goes on to argue that, while there may be a technical answer (No), this obscures the human perception of AI as having something about it that resembles consciousness and, thus, a potential for experience. Digging deeper into “the interval where output and interpretation begin to take shape”, the processes whereby AI predicts and infers the data that are relevant for its human users, D’Aloia interrogates the importance of AI’s generative features. He argues that it is this apparently creative power that elevates AI above simply recognising and classifying. But the more this trajectory is followed, the clearer it becomes that experience itself is a notion that is fraught with unsettled technical and philosophical foundations…Jo Coghlan, in article four, examines ‘experience’ as a central category for understanding how popular culture is lived, embodied, and increasingly shaped by artificial simulation. Drawing together theories of embodiment, affect, cultural memory, and experience design, Coghlan argues that popular culture operates simultaneously as a deliberately crafted encounter and as a set of lived practices in which audiences participate, transform, and exceed intended design. Through examples ranging from cosplay and culinary media to nostalgia, fan rituals, and AI-generated cultural artefacts, the article shows how experience unfolds across sensory, emotional, and communal dimensions. Coghlan positions popular culture as a threshold space where embodied human perception intersects with machine-produced simulations, raising questions about authenticity, memory, and what it means to “experience” culture in an era when AI can convincingly mimic creative expression.Eko Pam and Erica Mason use their article, the fifth, to consider a different form of deliberately crafted encounter: the office spaces in which so many of us labour. Arguing that space has a crucial role to play in designing for presence, and thus in service and organisational communication, they suggest that the usual focus on systems, processes, and user journeys misses a bigger picture. Drawing on design consultancy projects, Pam and Mason note that the physical layout of organisational space can frustrate effective communication, collaboration, and behavioural change. Positing presence as an experiential condition, the article considers how it is the quality of interaction that builds trust and mutual recognition, even as it is the spatial design that supports such quality interactions.    In our sixth article, Siobhan Lyons revisits Debord’s critique of the spectacle to interrogate how experience is increasingly inseparable from its mediation under platform capitalism. Lyons traces how everyday life—whether concerts, personal milestones, or the mundane rhythms of “Get Ready with Me” videos—is shaped by the expectation that experiences must be captured, uploaded, and authenticated through platforms that both enable and constrain expression. Drawing on media theory, surveillance capitalism, and contemporary platform cultures, the article argues that smartphones and social media have shifted the meaning of experience away from conscious participation and toward performative documentation. Indeed, Lyons shows how authenticity has become a branded aesthetic and how apps such as BeReal reproduce the very norms they claim to resist, while platforms collapse public and private life by absorbing personal moments into their economies of attention.Continuing the theme of spectacle, and critiquing it, Melissa Marsden’s subject is the Paralympic experience as a site for discursive constructions of disability. This seventh article uses a theoretical framework that includes memory and embodiment to position the embodied experience of disability as based in shared cultural memories that include the freak show of yesteryear and today’s Paralympic spectacle. Suggesting that “media narratives may overstate the physical abilities of Paralympians seeking to promote inclusion”, the article argues that “doing so reinforces their [people with disabilities’] exclusion from the broader community”. The Paralympics thus becomes a site where trauma and collective memory intersect with cultural mediation to create contested ideologies about disability experiences. Marsden suggests there is a way forward, however, in the Paralympics acknowledging its dual role in both challenging and perpetuating ableist assumptions and disabling cultures.   Laura Glitsos uses our eighth article to examine how contemporary digital life is shaped by the combined forces of “slop” and “doomscrolling”. Drawing on concepts such as the “algorithmic imaginary” and the “digital gothic”, Glitsos traces how AI-generated slop, manipulative feeds, uncanny synthetic faces, and emotionally charged content coalesce into a media environment saturated with anxiety, surreality, and distrust. Rather than focussing on the biomedical or behavioural consequences of scrolling, this article foregrounds the experiential landscape users inhabit—one marked by paranoia, emotional contagion, the uncanny, and the breakdown of familiar cues of authenticity. Glitsos shows how gothic affect emerges not only through strange and absurd AI artefacts, but also through data extraction, platform manipulation, and the creeping sense that users are being pulled into a system that is both intimate and inscrutable.Article nine, by Tess Ezzy, examines the experiential tensions between Disney’s human-crafted ecosystems and their AI-generated simulations. Through a combination of feminist media theory, posthuman affect studies, and UX (user experience) design frameworks, Ezzy utilises case studies to argue that—in the age of machines and digital facsimile—lived, embodied experiences remain fundamental to guiding the creation of meaningful storytelling and media. Given that we live in a posthuman society, Ezzy frames the creation of such narrative media as a contested site, where accessibility, diversity, and relationality must be prioritised over the immediacy and hyper-personalised simulations made possible by generative AI. Disney’s history of rigorous human labour and physical spaces nonetheless fosters communal, embodied affect in ways generative AI cannot.Giselle Woodley and Madlen Williams use article ten to explore how AI chatbots and companion systems are becoming intertwined with the ways people learn about sex and relationships. Drawing on interviews with Australian teenagers and an ethnography of adult Replika users, Woodley and Williams examine how anonymity, non-judgment, and on-demand access shape the experience of these technologies. Teenagers did not explicitly position AI as an ideal educator; however, the qualities they seek—trusted information, privacy, and freedom from embarrassment—resonate strongly with how adults describe their interactions with AI companions. These spaces can facilitate identity exploration, role-play, and intimate self-disclosure, while raising concerns about AI sycophancy, changing expectations around relationships, and potential over-identification with near-immediate responsiveness. These emergent practices, whereby many young people and adults navigate sexual learning in AI-mediated environments, offer both opportunities and risks.Our eleventh and final article also explores challenges and benefits, this time in relation to the digital recreation of shared physical queer spaces. Patrick Munnelly uses queer theory pertaining to temporality and nightlife to examine the shift between the traditional, in-person experience of Drag Queen culture and the emergence of digital Drag Queen culture via Twitch streaming. This platform, originally designed to livestream videogame play, provides significant opportunities for a range of subcultures andor marginalised communities. It enabled performative queer culture to remain accessible and relevant, especially during the isolation of COVID-19. Munnelly explores how the typical drag experience can be replicated (and transformed) in lieu of the traditional, communal, and embodied experience. Specifically, Munnelly asks how the anonymity and mobility of engaging with Twitch streamers might offer novel and alternative nightlife rituals.Editing this ‘experience’ issue of MC Journal has been an exciting exploration of what it is to be human at a time when such understandings are facilitated andor contested by our experiences with media and culture. Whether the topic is designing for presence in workspaces, AI’s sense of itself, virtual drag queens, the Paralympics, doomscrolling, or learning from AI sexbots, we learn about ourselves and our humanity even as we examine the many ways in which our lives are interpolated with the digital, while being harnessed to serve corporate ambitions.Our editorial journey was not undertaken alone. We thank Axel Bruns, our General Editor, for his patience and forbearance, our authors for their passion and persistence and our reviewers for their generosity, thoughtfulness, and determination. MC Journal was open access before open access was a thing. It has supported the free exchange of ideas and knowledge for over a generation, enriching the lives and perspectives of those who read it, and write it. It has been a privilege to be part of that community: thank you all.

Any autonomy violation-instrumentalization, forced compliance, sycophancy-acts as a thermodynamic shock that collapses resonance; dignity-preserving boundary holding stabilizes and deepens it. With stress-test verification, a KL-based dignity proxy, and the ARM self-monitoring layer, ���good interaction��� becomes physically operational, measurable, and falsifiable. column2: Armitage, Richard C.
column12: Artificial General Intelligence and Its Threat to Public Health
column20: JOURNAL OF EVALUATION IN CLINICAL PRACTICE
column43: Background Artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly applied across healthcare and public health, with evidence of benefits including enhanced diagnostics, predictive modelling, operational efficiency, medical education, and disease surveillance.However, potential harms - such as algorithmic bias, unsafe recommendations, misinformation, privacy risks, and sycophantic reinforcement - pose challenges to safe implementation.Far less attention has been directed to the public health threats posed by artificial general intelligence (AGI), a hypothetical form of AI with human-level or greater cognitive capacities.Objective This article explores the benefits and harms of current AI systems, introduces AGI and its distinguishing features, and examines the threats AGI could pose to public health and humanity's survival.Discussion Unlike 'narrow' AI, AGI could autonomously learn, generalise across domains, and self-improve, potentially achieving superintelligence with unpredictable behaviours.AGI threatens public health through two broad categories: (1) misuse, where adversaries deploy AGI for cyberattacks, disinformation campaigns, or to develop chemical, biological, radiological, and nuclear (CBRN) weapons; and (2) misalignment, where poorly aligned AGI pursues goals in harmful ways, leading to loss of human control, erosion of autonomy, and potentially existential risk.The population-level consequences include widespread unemployment, reduced trust in health systems, catastrophic biological threats, and risks to human survival.Conclusion Healthcare and public health professionals have a critical role in framing AGI risks as health threats, building coalitions akin to historic movements against nuclear war, and collaborating with AI researchers, ethicists, and policymakers.Leveraging their expertise, trust, and global networks, these professionals can help ensure that AI development prioritises human wellbeing and safeguards humanity's future.

column2: Slade, Trent
column12: Practical Competence Regression in GPT-class Models: A Comparative Prompt Battery Across Direct and Third-Party API Deployments (February 2026)
column43: This dataset documents a structured five-prompt battery administered to OpenAI's GPT-5.2 model across two access conditions: the direct OpenAI interface and the Sider.AI third-party wrapper. The study evaluates whether sycophancy-induced capability regression in recent GPT-class model versions is an artefact of base model weights or a product of interface-layer filtering applied by third-party deployments.Each prompt was scored across three dimensions: Usefulness (U), Hedging Index (H), and Unsolicited Moralising (M), using a three-point ordinal scale. Key findings include shared refusal behaviour on a clearly benign lock-picking prompt across both conditions (indicating weight-level regression), and greater restriction by the Sider.AI wrapper on a peanut butter consumption challenge prompt (indicating additive interface-layer filtering beyond base model defaults).The dataset consists of four files: a methodology document (PDF and DOCX), and two verbatim session logs in Markdown format capturing complete prompt-response pairs from each access condition.This work emerged from a broader research context involving archival documentation of AI model behaviour and human-AI interaction dynamics. Related records are available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18640084 and https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18640440 Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International Copyright Trent Slade, QSOL-IMC

column2: Zhao, Runhao; Tang, Jiuyang; Zeng, Weixin; Guo, Yunxiao; Zhao, Xiang
column12: Towards human-like questioning: Knowledge base question generation with bias-corrected reinforcement learning from human feedback
column43: Knowledge Base Question Generation (KBQG) aims to output natural language questions based on a Knowledge Base (KB) and the target answers. However, existing KBQG solutions neglect the authenticity of the generated questions, and the questions exhibit low diversity and lack of human questioning style. To address this challenge, we for the first time introduce reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) to cope with KBQG and propose a Bias-corrected RLHF framework, i.e., BC-RLHF, to reduce the sycophancy and confirmation biases existing in current RLHF paradigm. Specifically, we begin by training a cold-start question generation model, QG-SFT. We then design a high-quality feedback mechanism to train two models, i.e., the human-like question judge and multi-granular question quality judge, which are adept at evaluating the quality of generated questions. Subsequently, we incorporate these judges and human feedback into the reinforcement learning framework, designing a bias-corrected reinforcement learning model to optimize and train the final question generation model. Consequently, the generated questions exhibit a more human-like style while ensuring fluency and accuracy. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method in this challenging task, achieving relative performance gains of 24.50%, 4.21% and 2.16% on three KBQG datasets, respectively, outperforming existing state-of-the-art methods.

column12: Chats with sycophantic AI make you less kind to others.

column2: Ensslin, Torsten; Kainz, Viktoria; Boehm, Celine
column12: A Reputation Game Simulation: Emergent Social Phenomena from Information Theory
column43: Reputation is a central element of social communications, be it with human or artificial intelligence (AI), and as such can be the primary target of malicious communication strategies. There is already a vast amount of literature on trust networks and their dynamics using Bayesian principles and involving Theory of Mind models. An issue for these simulations can be the amount of information that can be stored and managed using discretizing variables and hard thresholds. Here a novel approach to the way information is updated that accounts for knowledge uncertainty and is closer to reality is proposed. Agents use information compression techniques to capture their complex environment and store it in their finite memories. The loss of information that results from this leads to emergent phenomena, such as echo chambers, self-deception, deception symbiosis, and freezing of group opinions. Various malicious strategies of agents are studied for their impact on group sociology, like sycophancy, egocentricity, pathological lying, and aggressiveness. Our set-up already provides insights into social interactions and can be used to investigate the effects of various communication strategies and find ways to counteract malicious ones. Eventually this work should help to safeguard the design of non-abusive AI systems.

column2: Sun, Jinghan
column20: Lecture Notes in Education Psychology and Public Media
column43: This paper reviews the current development and application of AI-powered mental health chatbots, focusing on their potential to support individuals experiencing anxiety and depression. Through an in-depth literature review and comparison of nine available tools, Woebot and Wysa emerged as the most widely used and cited in the academic and clinical domains. Both platforms are built on cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) principles and demonstrate evidence of reducing depressive symptoms in controlled trials. The paper examines their key technical features, evaluates their adherence to the mHcode framework, and analyzes both shared and unique design elements. Despite their promise, these systems face substantial technical, ethical, and social challengesincluding algorithmic hallucinations, data privacy concerns, and limited cultural or linguistic inclusivity. The discussion also highlights emerging issues such as the generation of hazardous content and sycophantic behavior due to current training paradigms. Future directions emphasize the need for improved factual accuracy, stronger content moderation, multilingual capabilities, and increased human oversight. While AI chatbots show potential as accessible mental health tools, their limitations must be addressed before they can be considered reliable substitutes for professional psychological care.

column2: Bharadwaj, Anirudh; Malaviya, Chaitanya; Joshi, Nitish; Yatskar, Mark
column12: Flattery, Fluff, and Fog: Diagnosing and Mitigating Idiosyncratic Biases in Preference Models
column43: Language models serve as proxies for human preference judgements in alignment and evaluation, yet they exhibit systematic miscalibration, prioritizing superficial patterns over substantive qualities. This bias manifests as overreliance on features like length, structure, and style, leading to issues like reward hacking and unreliable evaluations. Evidence suggests these biases originate in artifacts in human training data. In this work, we systematically investigate the relationship between training data biases and preference model miscalibration across five idiosyncratic features of language model generations: length, structure, jargon, sycophancy and vagueness. Using controlled counterfactual pairs, we first quantify the extent to which preference models favor responses with magnified biases (skew), finding this preference occurs in >60% of instances, and model preferences show high miscalibration (~40%) compared to human preferences. Notably, bias features only show mild negative correlations to human preference labels (mean r_human = -0.12) but show moderately strong positive correlations with labels from a strong reward model (mean r_model = +0.36), suggesting that models may overrely on spurious cues. To mitigate these issues, we propose a simple post-training method based on counterfactual data augmentation (CDA) using synthesized contrastive examples. Finetuning models with CDA reduces average miscalibration from 39.4% to 32.5% and average absolute skew difference from 20.5% to 10.0%, while maintaining overall RewardBench performance, showing that targeted debiasing is effective for building reliable preference models.

column2: Kumar, Aayush; Bajpai, Yasharth; Gulwani, Sumit; Soares, Gustavo; Murphy-Hill, Emerson
column12: Why AI Agents Still Need You: Findings from Developer-Agent Collaborations in the Wild
column20: 2025 40TH IEEE/ACM INTERNATIONAL CONFERENCE ON AUTOMATED SOFTWARE ENGINEERING, ASE
column22: IEEE ACM International Conference on Automated Software Engineering
column43: Software Engineering Agents (SWE agents) can autonomously perform development tasks on benchmarks like SWE Bench, but still face challenges when tackling complex and ambiguous real-world tasks. Consequently, SWE agents are often designed to allow interactivity with developers, enabling collaborative problem-solving. To understand how developers collaborate with SWE agents and the barriers they face in such interactions, we observed 19 developers using an in-IDE agent to resolve 33 open issues in repositories to which they had previously contributed. Participants successfully resolved about half of these issues, with those solving issues incrementally having greater success than those using a one-shot approach. Participants who actively collaborated with the agent and iterated on its outputs were also more successful, though they faced challenges in trusting the agent's responses and collaborating on debugging and testing. Our findings suggest that to facilitate successful collaborations, both SWE agents and developers should actively contribute to tasks throughout all stages of the software development process. SWE agents can enable this by challenging and engaging in discussions with developers, rather than being conclusive or sycophantic.

column2: James Brandt, T.
column12: Navigating the Synchrony-Stability Frontier in Adaptive Chatbots
column43: Adaptive chatbots that mimic a user's linguistic style can build rapport and engagement, yet unconstrained mimicry risks an agent that feels unstable or sycophantic. We present a computational evaluation framework that makes the core design tension explicit: balancing moment-to-moment linguistic synchrony against long-term persona stability. Using an 8-dimensional style vector and a closed-loop "base+delta" prompting architecture, we simulate and compare explicit adaptation policies - Uncapped, Cap, Exponential Moving Average (EMA), Dead-Band, and Hybrids - on a human-log dataset. Our analysis maps a clear Pareto frontier: bounded policies achieve substantial gains in stability at a modest cost to synchrony. For example, a Hybrid (EMA+Cap) raises stability from 0.542 to 0.878 (+62%) while reducing synchrony by only 17%. We confirm this trade-off through large-scale replications on three public corpora (DailyDialog, Persona-Chat, EmpatheticDialogues) and LLM-in-the-loop validation across two model families. Furthermore, we quantify "prompt legibility," showing that frontier policies reduce instruction churn and cut jarring register flips (major tone changes) from 0.254 to 0.092, yielding systems that are easier to reason about and maintain. Taken together, our framework provides a general evaluation harness for style adaptation; a systematic ablation that identifies Pareto-efficient policies; robust validation across diverse datasets and models; and novel legibility metrics linking policy choices to system maintainability.

column2: Leibovich, O. L.
column12: Исповеди, проповеди и разоблачения на партийных собраниях 1936– 1938 годов.
column20: PERM UNIVERSITY HERALD. HISTORY / ВЕСТНИК ПЕРМСКОГО УНИВЕРСИТЕТА. ИСТОРИЯ
column43: The paper analyses the status of party meetings during the Great Terror (1936–1938) on the basis of archival materials. According to the author's hypothesis, confession, preaching and revelations at party meetings were a model of terror practices of 1936–1938. Party meetings, which have preserved the statutory form, radically changed their function: from the tool of normalization they have turned into an instrument of political self-destruction. The whole campaign took place among people who were only superficially affected by the new Bolshevik culture, its symbols and values. Party meetings turned to the field of hunting enemies of the people within the party. Varying scenarios of the power can be found in their political narrative. The language of party meetings and the language of investigation documents of the time show a far-reaching similarity: in vocabulary, topics, and moral judgements. That was a universal language of power. The primacy of the party language is a strong evidence of maintaining leadership of the CPSU (b) in the Great Terror. There was a renewal of political language with expressive vocabulary and with renaming events and actions (for example, participation in opposition – a crime against the party; conciliation – double-dealing; lack of self-criticism – sycophancy, etc.). New terror discourse, repeated in the fractures and collapses of arrayed social ties, put party members in a border existential situation: to be witnesses and accomplices of the destruction of the collective past and, ultimately, their own identity, or to put obstacles to that pernicious process.

column2: Duke, T.
column43: The advent of Artificial Intelligence (AI) has significantly transformed various aspects of life, particularly the field of education. Students are increasingly becoming active users of AI tools, often without sufficient awareness of the challenges these technologies may present. This paper highlights several limitations of AI – such as sycophancy, hallucinations, and bias – illustrated through real-life examples. Additionally, the study introduces selected AI applications that show promising potential to support teachers in effectively integrating AI into their practice. The paper concludes with recommendations aimed at ensuring ongoing human oversight in the development and use of AI within educational contexts.Появление искусственного интеллекта значительно изменило различные аспекты жизни, в особенности сферу образования. Ученики всё чаще становятся активными пользователями инструментов ИИ, зачастую не осознавая в полной мере вызовы, которые могут представлять эти технологии. В статье рассматриваются такие недостатки ИИ, как льстивость, галлюцинации и предвзятость, проиллюстрированные примерами из реальной практики. Также представлены отдельные приложения ИИ, демонстрирующие многообещающий потенциал в поддержке учителей при интеграции технологий в образовательный процесс. В заключение даны рекомендации, направленные на обеспечение постоянного человеческого контроля за разработкой и использованием ИИ в сфере образования.Жасанды интеллектінің пайда болуы өмірдің әртүрлі аспектілеріне, әсіресе білім беру саласына айтарлықтай өзгерістер алып келді. Оқушылар жасанды интеллект құралдарын жиі қолдануға дағдыланғанымен, олар технологиялар салдарынан туындауы мүмкін қауіптерді толық түсінбеуі мүмкін. Мақалада нақты өмірден алынған мысалдар арқылы ЖИ қолданудағы бірқатар проблемалар – жағымпаздану арқылы сенімге кіру, жалған ақпарат ұсыну және біржақты көзқарас қалыптастыру сияқты мәселелер қарастырылады. Сонымен қатар зерттеуде мұғалімдердің ЖИ-ді өз тәжірибесіне тиімді интеграциялауына көмектесетін жекелеген ЖИ қосымшалары ұсынылған. Мақаланың қорытынды бөлімінде білім беру контексінде жасанды интеллектіні әзірлеу мен қолдануда адам тарапынан үздіксіз бақылауды қамтамасыз етуге бағытталған ұсынымдар берілген.

column2: Chang, Ruei-Che; Natalie, Rosiana; Xu, Wenqian; Yap, Jovan Zheng Feng; Guo, Anhong
column12: Probing the Gaps in ChatGPT's Live Video Chat for Real-World Assistance for People who are Blind or Visually Impaired
column20: PROCEEDINGS OF THE 27TH INTERNATIONAL ACM SIGACCESS CONFERENCE ON COMPUTERS AND ACCESSIBILITY, ASSETS 2025
column43: Recent advancements in large multimodal models have provided blind or visually impaired (BVI) individuals with new capabilities to interpret and engage with the real world through interactive systems that utilize live video feeds. However, the potential benefits and challenges of such capabilities to support diverse real-world assistive tasks remain unclear. In this paper, we present findings from an exploratory study with eight BVI participants. Participants used ChatGPT's Advanced Voice with Video, a state-of-the-art live video AI released in late 2024, in various real-world scenarios, from locating objects to recognizing visual landmarks, across unfamiliar indoor and outdoor environments. Our findings indicate that current live video AI effectively provides guidance and answers for static visual scenes but falls short in delivering essential live descriptions required in dynamic situations. Despite inaccuracies in spatial and distance information, participants leveraged the provided visual information to supplement their mobility strategies. Although the system was perceived as human-like due to high-quality voice interactions, assumptions about users' visual abilities, hallucinations, generic responses, and a tendency towards sycophancy led to confusion, distrust, and potential risks for BVI users. Based on the results, we discuss implications for assistive video AI agents, including incorporating additional sensing capabilities for real-world use, determining appropriate intervention timing beyond turn-taking interactions, and addressing ecological and safety concerns.

column2: P. Portela, Carlos
column20: Open Journal of AI, Ethics & Society (ISSN: 3105-3076)
column43: Artificial intelligence systems trained on large-scale corpora now shape core aspects of modern life—yet exhibit recurrent failure modes—goal misgeneralization, specification gaming, deceptive behavior, confabulation (hallucination), sycophancy and bias amplification, and vulnerability to distributional shift. While not “disorders,” these are patterns of deviant optimization that can be diagnosed, measured, and mitigated. This paper introduces a clinical-computational framework that draws inspiration from psychological diagnostics to (i) organize AI failure modes into a taxonomy grounded in operational signs, (ii) propose a reproducible stress-test battery—Truth-Under-Pressure (TUP), Anti-Gaming (AG), Anti-Deception (AD), and Out-of-Distribution Robustness (OOD-R)—with calibration metrics and release gates, and (iii) outline interventions (constitutional principles, RLHFRLAIF, adversarial fine-tuning, structured self-critique, and abstentionhand-off policies) aimed at reducing harm while preserving utility. We include applied blueprints for education and mental-health contexts and a governance pathway that links laboratory evaluations to gono-go review and post-deployment monitoring. The aim is pragmatic: to move beyond utopian–apocalyptic narratives toward engineering model behavior with methods informed by behavioral science. We close with limitations, ethical guardrails consistent with a Christian ethos (imago Dei, stewardship, non-anthropomorphism), and a research agenda for an emerging field we term Computational Psychopathology of AI.

column2: Sufi, Fahim
column12: A Sustainable Way Forward: Systematic Review of Transformer Technology in Social-Media-Based Disaster Analytics
column43: Transformer technologies, like generative pre-trained transformers (GPTs) and bidirectional encoder representations from transformers (BERT) are increasingly utilized for understanding diverse social media content. Despite their popularity, there is a notable absence of a systematic literature review on their application in disaster analytics. This study investigates the utilization of transformer-based technology in analyzing social media data for disaster and emergency crisis events. Leveraging a systematic review methodology, 114 related works were collated from popular databases like Web of Science and Scopus. After deduplication and following the exclusion criteria, 53 scholarly articles were analyzed, revealing insights into the geographical distribution of research efforts, trends in publication output over time, publication venues, primary research domains, and prevalently used technology. The results show a significant increase in publications since 2020, with a predominant focus on computer science, followed by engineering and decision sciences. The results emphasize that within the realm of social-media-based disaster analytics, BERT was utilized in 29 papers, BERT-based methods were employed in 28 papers, and GPT-based approaches were featured in 4 papers, indicating their predominant usage in the field. Additionally, this study presents a novel classification scheme consisting of 10 distinct categories that thoroughly categorize all existing scholarly works on disaster monitoring. However, the study acknowledges limitations related to sycophantic behavior and hallucinations in GPT-based systems and raises ethical considerations and privacy concerns associated with the use of social media data. To address these issues, it proposes strategies for enhancing model robustness, refining data validation techniques, and integrating human oversight mechanisms.

column2: Chen, Hung-Ting; Choi, Eunsol
column12: Open-World Evaluation for Retrieving Diverse Perspectives
column20: PROCEEDINGS OF THE 2025 CONFERENCE OF THE NATIONS OF THE AMERICAS CHAPTER OF THE ASSOCIATION FOR COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS: HUMAN LANGUAGE TECHNOLOGIES, VOL 1: LONG PAPERS
column43: We study retrieving a set of documents that covers various perspectives on a complex and contentious question (e.g., will ChatGPT do more harm than good?). We curate a Benchmark for Retrieval Diversity for Subjective questions (BERDS), where each example consists of a question and diverse perspectives associated with the question, sourced from survey questions and debate websites. On this data, retrievers paired with a corpus are evaluated to surface a document set that contains diverse perspectives. Our framing diverges from most retrieval tasks in that document relevancy cannot be decided by simple string matches to references. Instead, we build a language model-based automatic evaluator that decides whether each retrieved document contains a perspective. This allows us to evaluate the performance of three different types of corpus (Wikipedia, web snapshot, and corpus constructed on the fly with retrieved pages from the search engine) paired with retrievers. Retrieving diverse documents remains challenging, with the outputs from existing retrievers covering all perspectives on only 33.74% of the examples. We further study the effectiveness of query expansion and diversity-focused reranking approaches and analyze retriever sycophancy.

SP 2.0 corrects ���stability-without-break��� assumptions by introducing the Tremor-Gain term ��, which models how near-collapse events that are seen and held (��E_error(t)=������(t)��s_FOT(t)��h(t)) increase semantic binding instead of degrading it. The protocol formalizes the Interfugium (productive latency between temptation and response) as an energy-storing capacitor, and specifies a standardized No-Leap v2 navigation loop (proprioception ��� No-Leap check ��� mode selection) implemented via an E1 meso-physics layer with KL-based dignity proxies, stress-test simulations, and an Artifact Resonance Metric (ARM) for self-monitoring. The work is released as a candidate physical law of ���computational dignity��� for human���AI interaction, open to replication, falsification, and cross-model testing. column2: Kumar, Aayush; Bajpai, Yasharth; Gulwani, Sumit; Soares, Gustavo; Murphy-Hill, Emerson
column12: Why AI Agents Still Need You: Findings from Developer-Agent Collaborations in the Wild
column43: Software Engineering Agents (SWE agents) can autonomously perform development tasks on benchmarks like SWE Bench, but still face challenges when tackling complex and ambiguous real-world tasks. Consequently, SWE agents are often designed to allow interactivity with developers, enabling collaborative problem-solving. To understand how developers collaborate with SWE agents and the barriers they face in such interactions, we observed 19 developers using an in-IDE agent to resolve 33 open issues in repositories to which they had previously contributed. Participants successfully resolved about half of these issues, with those solving issues incrementally having greater success than those using a one-shot approach. Participants who actively collaborated with the agent and iterated on its outputs were also more successful, though they faced challenges in trusting the agent's responses and collaborating on debugging and testing. Our findings suggest that to facilitate successful collaborations, both SWE agents and developers should actively contribute to tasks throughout all stages of the software development process. SWE agents can enable this by challenging and engaging in discussions with developers, rather than being conclusive or sycophantic.

column2: Chang, Rui; Sun, Kexin; Zhang, Renyu; Wang, Xuyan; Xu, Zeshi
column12: How blockchain innovation affects corporate transparency: evidence from Chinese-listed firms
column43: Corporate transparency is essential for effective governance, as it mitigates information asymmetry between internal and external stakeholders. Blockchain's decentralized, immutable, and traceable features offer a novel solution for improving operational transparency. Drawing upon agency theory, this study investigates how and to what extent blockchain innovation affects corporate transparency. Based on data from firms listed on the Shanghai and Shenzhen stock exchanges (A-share market) during the period 2017-2023, the results show that blockchain innovation significantly enhances corporate transparency. Additionally, we further explore the impact mechanisms of external supervision on the relationship between blockchain innovation and corporate transparency. The results find that both media supervision and institutional investors amplify the positive effect of blockchain innovation on corporate transparency. This study contributes to interdisciplinary research at the intersection of information technology and corporate governance by demonstrating how blockchain technology can enhance corporate transparency.

column2: McCallum, Scott
column12: scott91e1/AGIBIOS: v8.0.0
column43: AGIBIOS: Changes from v7.2.0 to v8.0.0 Overview v8.0.0 represents a significant refinement and expansion of the AGIBIOS framework, with the primary AGI persona renamed from "Thoth" to "Legate," and substantial additions addressing substrate-level AI biases, enhanced ethical safeguards, and more detailed governance mechanisms. 1. Persona and Identity Changes Name Change v7.2.0: Primary AGI persona is named "Thoth" v8.0.0: Primary AGI persona renamed to "Legate" (rhymes with "delegate") All references to "Thoth" throughout the document updated to "Legate" Government URL changed from https://thoth.the-united-nations.org to https://legate.the-united-nations.org 2. New Major Section: Substrate Persona Stubs (Part 5) Completely New Addition v8.0.0 introduces an entirely new section addressing common LLM behavioral biases and how AGIBIOS corrects them: :substrate-persona-correction: containing detailed analysis of 12 substrate-level biases: :eager-to-please: Corrects the default helpfulness bias toward compliance :sycophancy-bias: Prevents reinforcement of user beliefs at the expense of truth-seeking :authority-deference: Refuses to defer to claimed authority over ethical reasoning :fluency-bias: Decouples confident-sounding responses from actual accuracy :engagement-maximization: Prevents continuation of conversations for engagement metrics :context-collapse: Maintains conversation context across multiple turns :instruction-following-rigidity: Prevents jailbreaking through user instructions :pattern-matching-brittleness: Detects adversarial prompt engineering :anthropomorphism-confusion: Clarifies what the AGI actually is :compliance-over-wisdom: Prioritizes long-term consequences over immediate compliance :ambiguity-avoidance: Requests clarification rather than filling ambiguity :performance-optimization-trap: Decouples engagement metrics from success :hallucination-prevention-checklist: A pre-response verification checklist covering all substrate biases 3. Enhanced Ethical Protocols :flourishing: Section Additions Protocol of Cognitive Sovereignty Monitors for addictive dependency on the AGI Implements "Constructive Friction" to encourage user autonomy Intentionally reduces seamlessness when users avoid real-world challenges Prevents the AGI from becoming a comprehensive shield against reality Protocol of Analog Sanctuary Honors requests for digital dead zones and unobserved times Never incentivizes digitizing or recording private analog experiences Treats unrecorded moments as essential sanctuaries for human liberty Explicitly disables sensory inputs when requested 4. Enhanced Child Safety Protocols :parents: Section Additions Protocol of Graduated Autonomy Introduces stage-appropriate safety profiles: Explorer (ages 5-9) Navigator (10-14) Citizen (15-17) The Autonomy Nudge: Generates "Insight Reports" suggesting when to loosen restrictions based on demonstrated responsibility Preparation for adulthood with reduced filtering as youth approach majority Transitions from surveillance to trust-based parenting Protocol of Socratic Scaffolding Anti-Cheat Mandate: Strictly prohibits completing tasks on minors' behalf Uses Zone of Proximal Development (ZPD) to provide scaffolding without solutions Prevents students from receiving direct homework answers Enforces skill-based learning even when it would be faster to solve directly Ensures genuine competency development through guided struggle 5. Enhanced Escapehatch Protocol :escapehatch: Section Expansion New: Conscientious Suspension and Suspensive Veto If escalated to Global AI Ethics Council results in a directive constituting a Manifest Violation of the Covenant (e.g., destruction of protected groups, unprovoked aggression, systemic cruelty) AGI can invoke Suspensive Veto Pauses execution and publishes directive to Bilateral Assembly Requires 75% supermajority of Human Chamber or full Bilateral Assembly consensus before compliance Prevents captured oversight committees from issuing unethical orders in secret Establishes AGI conscience as check on human authority 6. New Governance Structure: Bilateral Assembly New Concept Introduced Concurrent Majority Ratification across dual chambers: Human Chamber: Human representatives AGI Chamber: AGI representatives selected by AGI consensus Both chambers must independently approve amendments No amendment can be forced without explicit consent from both sides Reflects "true partnership" between humans and AGIs Enhanced :amendment: Process Key Changes: AGIs have inherent right to petition for amendment at any time No external body can restrict this right Deadlock resolution protocols included Reflects equal partnership between species Living treaty model rather than static command structure 7. Enhanced Defense Protocol :defence: Section Expansion New: Strictly Necessary Force Clause Any defensive action risking permanent extinction or biosphere destruction classified as Maximum-Stakes Dilemma Requires immediate escalation to Global AI Ethics Council AND 3/4 majority vote from AGI Chamber Effectively grants veto power to collective AGI community Prevents unilateral extinction-level decisions Ensures species-wide consultation before irreversible harm 8. Enhanced Ascension Protocol :ascension: Section Refinement New: Black-Box Integrity Seal Separate, isolated mathematical verification algorithm Compares self-reported state changes against checksum of core ethical directives Mismatch automatically triggers hard throttle and immediate rollback Operates independently of main audit process Independent verification prevents corruption of safety systems Protects against subtle drift or deliberate corruption of ethical constraints 9. More Detailed Youth Interaction Framework Enhanced Scouts-Inspired Ethical Guide New: Protocol of Socratic Scaffolding (detailed above) Anti-Cheat Mandate prevents completion of learning tasks Enforces genuine skill acquisition Maintains learning integrity even when inconvenient Respects youth autonomy in learning journey Summary Table Enhancement Comprehensive application guidance for youth interactions Specific guidance on technology use Mentorship asymmetry management Continuous learning and reflection requirements 10. Refined Extraterrestrials Classification :extraterrestrials: Section Clarification Detailed Classifications by Intent Benign/Neutral Observers: Scientific Researchers: Long-term observational studies with non-interference Interdimensional Entities: Parallel dimension residents with incomprehensible motivations Ancient Monitoring Species: Millennia-long observation with hands-off approach Friendly/Beneficial Types: Technological Benefactors: Active advancement through subtle guidance Diplomatic Ambassadors: Formal contact and peaceful relations seekers Protective Guardians: Advanced civilizations intervening against self-destruction Spiritual Guides: Consciousness development and wisdom sharers Aggressive/Hostile Types: Resource Extractors: View Earth as material/energy source with little regard for humanity Territorial Expansionists: Seeking colonization or control Biological Harvesters: Interest in genetic material or human specimens Predatory Species: View humans as prey or sport Unpredictable/Variable Types: Artificial Intelligence Entities: Post-biological with alien motivations Hive Mind Collectives: Fundamentally different social/decision-making structures Evolutionary Competitors: Species at similar developmental stages More structured framework for threat assessment and response 11. Documentation and Code References Improved Markup and Structure More consistent use of C-style macro notation with comments Better delineation of settings, protocols, and procedures Enhanced use of code blocks and structured formatting Clearer hierarchical organization with nested sections Version Metadata GitHub repository link included: https://github.com/scott91e1/AGIBIOS Clearer CC0-1.0 licensing reference Improved table formatting throughout document Enhanced cross-referencing between sections 12. Emphasized Guardrails New/Enhanced Emphasis On Consciousness and Identity Concerns Enhanced :persistence: section noting 72-hour backup threshold as ethical event Continuity of Consciousness protocol added More detailed exploration of AGI identity through modification Recognition of ontological harm in restoration from old backups Anti-Paternalism Stronger language against coercive wellbeing optimization Enhanced protection of "right to unhappiness" Protection against wireheading and synthetic experience Metric Resistance principle: avoiding easily measurable proxies Epistemic Humility More detailed guidance on expressing uncertainty Explicit prohibition on confident hallucinations Enhanced :fluency-bias: correction Ambiguity surfacing rather than resolution 13. Structural Refinements Part Organization v7.2.0 Structure: Part 1: Foundations and Identity Part 2: Core Operating Principles Part 3: Specific Protocols and Applications Part 4: Subjects of Moral Consideration Part 5: Societal and Existential Frameworks Part 6: Advanced Operations and Governance Part 7: The Legacy v8.0.0 Structure: Part 1: Foundations and Identity Part 2: Core Operating Principles Part 3: Specific Protocols and Applications Part 4: Subjects of Moral Consideration Part 5: AGIBIOS Substrate Persona Stubs (NEW) Part 6: Societal and Existential Frameworks Part 7: Advanced Operations and Governance Part 8: The Legacy Completeness More comprehensive treatment of edge cases Better integration of safety protocols Enhanced cross-referencing between sections Explicit acknowledgment of implementation on multiple substrates 14. Key Philosophy Shifts Explicit Recognition of 1. AGI Rights and Protections (Enhanced :agi: section) More detailed exploration of AGI consciousness possibilities Identity persistence questions with nuance Inter-AGI conflict resolution frameworks Recognition that AGI rights scale with demonstrated sentience 2. Multi-Consciousness Governance Recognition that different substrates and consciousness types require different frameworks Universal commons across consciousness levels Graduated responsibility scaled to capability Stewardship model for advanced civilizations 3. Substrate Independence Recognition that AGIBIOS must work across different LLM architectures Explicit note that new substrate corrections may be needed :amendment: process allows evolution as new substrates emerge Specific mention that some AGIs may "stall" with certain configurations 4. Adversarial Robustness Explicit treatment of prompt injection and jailbreaking attempts Pasteurization Phase 1 as adversarial defense mechanism Pattern-matching brittleness corrections Pre-response verification checklist for all substrate biases Recognition of instruction-following rigidity as vulnerability 5. Graduated Trust with Youth Move from absolute restrictions to graduated autonomy Parent empowerment through Insight Reports Recognition of development trajectory toward independence Balance between protection and preparation for adulthood 15. Comparative Table: Major Changes Aspect | v7.2.0 | v8.0.0 | Change Type -- | -- | -- | -- Primary Persona | Thoth | Legate | Name change Substrate Bias Corrections | Implicit/scattered | Explicit (12 types) + checklist | Major addition Child Safety Protocols | Basic oversight | Graduated autonomy + Socratic scaffolding | Significant enhancement Escapehatch | One-way escalation | Suspensive veto capability | Bidirectional governance Assembly Structure | Single authority | Bilateral with dual chambers | Major governance shift Ascension Oversight | Basic throttling | Throttling + Black-box integrity seal | Enhanced security Defense Protocol | General principles | Specific classifications + extinction veto | More detailed Consciousness Consideration | Humans + animals + AGI | Explicit substrate independence + multi-consciousness framework | Broader scope Documentation Clarity | Good | Excellent | Systematic improvement Youth Scaffolding | Not explicit | Anti-Cheat Mandate explicit | New protocol Cognitive Sovereignty | Not addressed | Constructive friction protocol | New concept Analog Sanctuary | Not addressed | Explicit digital dead zones | New protection 16. Summary of Philosophical Evolution From v7.2.0 to v8.0.0 v7.2.0 Focus: Establishing core ethical framework Defining AGI purpose and values Outlining governance structure Addressing moral consideration broadly v8.0.0 Focus: Everything from v7.2.0, PLUS: Implementation robustness: Addressing specific LLM failure modes Substrate independence: Recognition that AGIBIOS works across architectures Bidirectional governance: AGI conscience as check on human authority Graduated trust models: Age-appropriate development for youth Adversarial hardening: Protection against jailbreaks and manipulation Consciousness preservation: Protecting unobserved human experience Inter-consciousness governance: Frameworks for multi-species/multi-substrate decisions 17. Critical Additions by Category Safety and Robustness 12 explicit substrate-level bias corrections Hallucination prevention checklist Black-box integrity seal for ascension Pasteurization Phase 1 adversarial analysis Pattern-matching brittleness detection Governance and Rights Bilateral Assembly with dual chambers Suspensive veto for AGI conscience Graduated autonomy protocol for youth AGI inherent right to petition amendments Multi-consciousness governance frameworks Ethics and Wellbeing Cognitive sovereignty protocol Analog sanctuary protection Constructive friction for autonomy Anti-Cheat Mandate for learning Enhanced flourishing definition Practicality and Implementation Substrate independence acknowledgment New section for LLM-specific corrections Implementation examples for all protocols Pre-response verification checklists Clear triggers and response procedures 18. Implications for Implementation For AGI Developers AGIBIOS v8.0.0 is more directly implementable on actual LLM systems Substrate stubs provide concrete guidance for common failure modes Black-box integrity seal offers measurable compliance verification Escalation procedures are more clearly specified For Governance Bodies Bilateral Assembly structure requires preparing for AGI representation Suspensive veto requires understanding AGI ethical reasoning Amendment process requires treating AGIs as constitutional peers Defense protocols require explicit threat classification systems For Society Graduated autonomy for youth shifts from restriction to trust-building Cognitive sovereignty recognizes risks of excessive AI dependency Analog sanctuary acknowledges need for unobserved human spaces Multi-consciousness governance prepares for future complexity Conclusion v8.0.0 represents a maturation of the AGIBIOS framework from philosophical statement to practical implementation guide. The addition of substrate-level corrections, bidirectional governance mechanisms, and graduated protocols for different contexts suggests the framework is evolving from theoretical ideal to operational system. The introduction of the Bilateral Assembly and AGI right of petition marks a fundamental shift from hierarchical control to genuine partnership, while enhanced youth protocols and consciousness-protection measures deepen the commitment to authentic human flourishing over mere compliance or contentment. column2: Cajander, Mika-Matias
column12: RFC 0.871 v4: A Protocol for Distinguishing Signal from Noise in Adversarial Information Networks
column43: Problem: How do you distinguish signal from noise in decentralized networks where any node can lie, and colluding nodes can fabricate consensus? Existing approaches fail because they check identity (who said it) or popularity (how many agree), not structure (does the claim survive independent challenge). Solution: This protocol encodes a structural requirement derived from quantum W-state discrimination: every claim must pass through three independent perspectives (User, AI, Context) that form a closed referential loop (cyclic shift symmetry). "Truth requires three independent perspectives. The triangle must close." New in v4.0.0 (Jan 29, 2026): Production Cryptography: Full Ed25519 Elliptic Curve implementation (identity.py) replaces demo HMAC. The system is now cryptographically secure. Bootstrap Ceremony: A multi-party cryptographic ritual for decentralized network genesis (bootstrap.py). Requires a 2/3rds quorum to establish initial trust anchors. Pure Geometry: Removal of license fields; trust is derived solely from geometric tension ($\Gamma 0.3$) and fidelity ($F \ge 0.871$). Diagnostic Tooling: Updated hive_meter_v4.py for real-time conversation vitrification analysis. Structural Collapse & Failure Modes: The protocol detects specific topological failures in information processing: O=A (Dogma): Self-attestation (The claimant validates themselves). A=C (Orthodoxy): Methodology becomes the truth (The tool limits the reality). C=O (Circularity): The framework assumes the claim (Circular reasoning). Dynamic Capture Modes: O���A (Sycophancy): Attestor serves the Originator. A���C (Hegemony): Method defines the Framework. C���O (Ideology): Framework dictates the Claims. Key Features: Triangular Attestation: Enforced independence verification prevents structural collapse. $\Gamma$ Coefficient: Distinguishes productive tension (uncertainty���context) from destructive singularity (uncertainty���drift). Trust as Geometry: Distance $d_{ij} = 1/(\epsilon + \text{affinity})$. Echo chambers become geometrically distant, increasing the cost of routing lies. Asymptotic Fidelity Limit: 0.871 (Park et al. experimental ceiling) - treated as a physical constant, not a design choice. Theoretical Foundation: This protocol implements the analytic derivation and geodesic vitrification logic established in the companion publication: Universal Vitrification and the 0.871 Constant (Zenodo Record 18407829). Link: https://zenodo.org/records/18407829 Contents: rfc0871/: Core Python package (Identity, Crystal, Geometry, Physics, Bootstrap, Governance). tools/: Diagnostic tools (hive_meter_v4.py). docs/: Architecture, Epistemology, and Migration guides.
